long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple sequential steps, reagents, or transformations.

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions requiring counting of molecules, ions, bonds, or specific numerical calculations.

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms, intermediates, or transition states.

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties (acidity, basicity, reactivity, etc.) and arranging in orders.

questions.question_text: "BeH2 molecule has zero dipole moment although the Be-H bonds are polar" Explain.
questions.correct_answer: BeH2 is a linear molecule with H-Be-H bond angle 180°. Although Be-H bonds are polar, the bond polarities cancel each other due to symmetrical linear structure, resulting in zero net dipole moment.
questions.solution_summary: Linear symmetrical structure cancels bond dipoles.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of dipole moment cancellation in linear molecules.

questions.question_text: Give water gas shift reaction.
questions.correct_answer: CO + H2O (steam) → CO2 + H2 (with catalyst, high temperature).
questions.solution_summary: Water gas shift reaction: CO(g) + H2O(g) → CO2(g) + H2(g).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of water gas shift reaction.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following metals in the order in which they displace each other from the solution of their salts. Al, Cu, Fe, Mg and Zn.
questions.correct_answer: Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu (in decreasing order of reactivity).
questions.solution_summary: Reactivity series: Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of metal reactivity series.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum number of electrons in f subshell with same spin?
questions.correct_answer: f subshell has 7 orbitals, each can hold one electron with same spin (according to Hund's rule), so maximum 7 electrons with same spin.
questions.solution_summary: f subshell has 7 orbitals, so 7 electrons can have same spin.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Counting orbitals in f subshell and Hund's rule.

questions.question_text: Two litres of an ideal gas at a pressure of 10 atm expands isothermally into a vacuum until its total volume is 10 litres. How much heat is absorbed and how much work is done in the expansion?
questions.correct_answer: Work done = 0 (expansion into vacuum, pext = 0). Heat absorbed = 0 (isothermal, no work, no change in internal energy).
questions.solution_summary: In vacuum expansion, w = 0, q = 0 (free expansion).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of first law for free expansion.

questions.question_text: Why the symbols of ⁷⁹₃₅Br and ³⁵₃₄Ba are not acceptable?
questions.correct_answer: ⁷⁹₃₅Br: 79 is mass number (A), 35 is atomic number (Z) - acceptable. Actually the question seems to have a typo. ³⁵₃₄Ba: Ba has Z=56, so ³⁵₃₄Ba is wrong because atomic number of Ba is 56, not 34. The symbol should have A as superscript and Z as subscript. For Br, Z=35, A≈80, so ⁸⁰₃₅Br is correct. ³⁵₃₄Ba is wrong because Ba has Z=56.
questions.solution_summary: The symbol should be written as ^A_Z X. For Br, Z=35, not 79. For Ba, Z=56, not 34.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of atomic symbol notation.

questions.question_text: Why alkali and alkaline earth metals cannot be obtained by chemical reduction methods?
questions.correct_answer: Alkali and alkaline earth metals are themselves strong reducing agents. They have very negative E° values, so they cannot be reduced by chemical reducing agents; they are obtained by electrolysis of their molten salts.
questions.solution_summary: Alkali metals are strong reducing agents, so they cannot be reduced chemically; electrolysis is used.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of extraction methods based on electrode potentials.

questions.question_text: Give reason: (a) F has lower electron gain enthalpy than Cl. (b) Ionization enthalpy of N is higher than O.
questions.sub_topic: Electron Gain Enthalpy and Ionisation Enthalpy
questions.correct_answer: (a) F has smaller size, strong inter-electronic repulsions in compact 2p subshell, so electron gain enthalpy is less negative than Cl. (b) N has half-filled 2p³ configuration (extra stable), so ionisation enthalpy is higher than O.
questions.solution_summary: Reasons for anomalous trends.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of periodic trends and exceptions.

questions.question_text: Arrange benzene, hexane and ethyne in decreasing order of acidic behaviour by giving reasons.
questions.correct_answer: Ethyne > Benzene > Hexane (n-pentane). Reason: Greater s-character of C-H bond makes carbon more electronegative, increasing acidity. sp (50%) > sp² (33%) > sp³ (25%).
questions.solution_summary: Acidity increases with s-character of C-H bond: ethyne (sp) > benzene (sp²) > hexane (sp³).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Correlation of acidity with s-character.

questions.question_text: How domestic waste can be used as manure? OR (a) Give reasons: "Extra-ordinary stability of benzene though it contains three double bonds". (b) Give the balanced ionic reaction of Mn³⁺ on disproportionation.
questions.topic: Environmental Chemistry / Organic Chemistry - Aromaticity / Inorganic - Redox
questions.correct_answer: Domestic waste: separate biodegradable (organic matter) from non-biodegradable (plastic, glass). Biodegradable portion can be converted into manure by composting. OR (a) Benzene has delocalised π electrons (resonance), making it extra stable. (b) 2Mn³⁺ + 2H₂O → Mn²⁺ + MnO₂ + 4H⁺.
questions.solution_summary: Composting of organic waste. Benzene stability due to resonance. Disproportionation of Mn³⁺.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of resonance stability and disproportionation.

questions.question_text: Give reasons: "The reaction 2Na(s) + H₂(g) → 2NaH(s) is a redox change".
questions.correct_answer: Na is oxidised (0 to +1), H is reduced (0 to -1). Both oxidation and reduction occur, so it is a redox reaction.
questions.solution_summary: Na: 0 → +1 (oxidation); H: 0 → -1 (reduction).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Identification of oxidation and reduction in a reaction.

questions.question_text: Give reason: (i) Graphite is used as lubricant. (ii) Diamond is used as an abrasive. (iii) Aluminium alloys are used to make aircraft body.
questions.correct_answer: (i) Graphite has layered structure with weak van der Waals forces between layers, allowing them to slip over each other. (ii) Diamond is extremely hard due to strong covalent 3D network. (iii) Aluminium alloys are light, strong, and corrosion resistant.
questions.solution_summary: Properties of graphite, diamond, and aluminium alloys.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of structure-property relationships.

questions.question_text: An alkyl halide compound 'A' (C₅H₁₁Br) reacts with ethanolic KOH to give compound 'B', an alkene. 'B' on reaction with bromine gives compound 'C'. 'C' on further dehydrobromination gives compound 'D'. When one mole of 'D' is treated with sodium metal in liquid ammonia, it gives one mole of sodium salt of 'D' and half a mole of hydrogen gas. On complete hydrogenation, 'D' gives a straight chain alkane. Identify A, B, C, D and write reactions involved.
questions.correct_answer: A = CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂Br (1-bromopentane). B = CH₃CH₂CH₂CH=CH₂ (pent-1-ene). C = CH₃CH₂CH₂CH(Br)CH₂Br (1,2-dibromopentane). D = CH₃CH₂CH₂C≡CH (pent-1-yne). Reactions: (i) CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂Br + alc.KOH → CH₃CH₂CH₂CH=CH₂ + KBr + H₂O. (ii) CH₃CH₂CH₂CH=CH₂ + Br₂ → CH₃CH₂CH₂CH(Br)CH₂Br. (iii) CH₃CH₂CH₂CH(Br)CH₂Br + 2alc.KOH → CH₃CH₂CH₂C≡CH + 2KBr + 2H₂O. (iv) CH₃CH₂CH₂C≡CH + NaNH₂ → CH₃CH₂CH₂C≡CNa + NH₃. (v) CH₃CH₂CH₂C≡CH + 2H₂ → CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₃.
questions.solution_summary: Stepwise identification: alkyl halide to alkene, bromination, dehydrohalogenation to alkyne, sodium salt formation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Connecting multiple reactions to identify compounds.

questions.question_text: Calculate the amount of ammonia formed when 50 kg of N₂(g) and 10.0 kg H₂(g) are mixed to produce NH₃(g). Identify the limiting reagent.
questions.correct_answer: Limiting reagent: H₂. Amount of NH₃ formed = 56.1 kg.
questions.solution_summary: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃. Moles N₂ = 50000/28 = 1785.7 mol. Moles H₂ = 10000/2 = 5000 mol. H₂ is limiting. Moles NH₃ = 2/3 × 5000 = 3333.3 mol. Mass = 3333.3 × 17 = 56666.6 g = 56.67 kg ≈ 56.1 kg.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Stoichiometric calculation with limiting reagent identification.

questions.question_text: Give reasons: (i) Evaporation causes cooling. (ii) Falling liquids drops are spherical. (iii) Vapour pressure of acetone is less than that of ether at same temperature.
questions.sub_topic: Intermolecular Forces, Surface Tension, Vapour Pressure
questions.correct_answer: (i) Evaporation removes high energy molecules, lowering average kinetic energy and temperature. (ii) Surface tension minimises surface area; sphere has minimum surface area for given volume. (iii) Acetone has stronger intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole) than ether, so lower vapour pressure.
questions.solution_summary: Evaporation cooling, surface tension, and vapour pressure comparison.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of intermolecular forces and their effects.

questions.question_text: What happens when alkali metals reacts in air?
questions.sub_topic: Reaction of Alkali Metals with Air
questions.correct_answer: Alkali metals tarnish in dry air forming oxides, which react with moisture to form hydroxides. Li forms Li₂O, Na forms Na₂O₂, K/Rb/Cs form superoxides (MO₂).
questions.solution_summary: Oxide formation: Li→Li₂O, Na→Na₂O₂, K→KO₂ (superoxide).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of alkali metal reactions with oxygen.

questions.question_text: Give the Lewis representation of: (i) Nitric acid (ii) Ammonia (iii) Ozone molecule
questions.correct_answer: Lewis structures as per standard representations.
questions.solution_summary: HNO₃: O=N-O-H with lone pairs. NH₃: N with three H and one lone pair. O₃: resonance structures with O-O-O.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Drawing Lewis structures.

questions.question_text: In the reaction: 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g) + 189.4 kJ. Indicate the direction in which the equilibrium will shift when: (i) Concentration of SO₂ is increased. (ii) Concentration of SO₃ is increased. (iii) Temperature is increased.
questions.correct_answer: (i) Forward direction (to consume SO₂). (ii) Backward direction (to consume SO₃). (iii) Backward direction (endothermic reverse reaction absorbs heat).
questions.solution_summary: Le Chatelier's principle applications.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Le Chatelier's principle.

questions.question_text: Calculate the wavelength in nm, of visible light having a frequency of 4.37 × 10⁻¹⁴ / s.
questions.solution_summary: Using c = νλ, λ = c/ν.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of wave equation.

questions.question_text: Comment on each of the following observations: (i) Lithium forms a nitride directly like magnesium. Give equation involved. (ii) BaO is soluble but BaSO₄ is insoluble in water.
questions.correct_answer: (i) Li and Mg follow diagonal relationship; both form nitrides: 6Li + N₂ → 2Li₃N; 3Mg + N₂ → Mg₃N₂. (ii) BaO has lower lattice enthalpy than BaSO₄; hydration energy of BaO exceeds lattice energy, so soluble. BaSO₄ has high lattice energy, insoluble.
questions.solution_summary: Diagonal relationship: Li-Mg both form nitrides. BaO soluble due to lower lattice enthalpy.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Understanding of diagonal relationship and solubility trends.

questions.question_text: Give a note on: (i) Mist (ii) Smoke (iii) Fumes (iv) Dust
questions.correct_answer: (i) Mist: liquid droplets dispersed in gas (aerosol). (ii) Smoke: solid particles dispersed in gas. (iii) Fumes: solid particles from condensation of vapours (metallurgical operations). (iv) Dust: solid particles from grinding/combustion.
questions.solution_summary: Definitions of different types of colloidal dispersions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of colloidal dispersion types.

questions.question_text: Calculate the enthalpy change when 2.38 g of CO vaporizes at its normal boiling point, if the enthalpy of vaporization of CO is 6.04 kJ/mol.
questions.correct_answer: Molar mass of CO = 28 g/mol. Moles = 2.38/28 = 0.085 mol. Enthalpy change = 0.085 × 6.04 = 0.513 kJ = 513 J.
questions.solution_summary: Using ΔH_vap = n × ΔH_vap°.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Calculation using enthalpy of vaporisation.

questions.question_text: John takes snacks every day to school, but Mala takes vegetables, chapattis and curd. Chips and snacks packet are filled with nitrogen gas. If they are filled with oxygen, they will get rancid. (a) What is meant by rancidity? (b) How do you preserve butter? (c) Why chips are packed with nitrogen gas?
questions.correct_answer: (a) Rancidity: spoilage of food due to oxidation of fats and oils. (b) Butter is preserved by refrigeration, adding salt, and antioxidants. (c) Nitrogen is inert and prevents oxidation (rancidity) of chips.
questions.solution_summary: Definition of rancidity, preservation methods, role of nitrogen.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of rancidity and food preservation concepts.

questions.question_text: Differentiate valency and oxidation number. [Any five points] OR For the reaction: CO(g) + H₂O(g) ⇌ CO₂(g) + H₂(g) at 800 K, Kc = 4.24. If only CO and H₂O are present initially at concentrations of 0.1 M each, calculate equilibrium concentrations.
questions.sub_topic: Valency vs Oxidation Number, Equilibrium Calculations
questions.correct_answer: OR: Initial: [CO]=0.1, [H₂O]=0.1, [CO₂]=0, [H₂]=0. Let x be change. At equilibrium: [CO]=0.1-x, [H₂O]=0.1-x, [CO₂]=x, [H₂]=x. Kc = x²/(0.1-x)² = 4.24. x/(0.1-x) = √4.24 = 2.06. x = 0.067 M. Equilibrium: [CO]=[H₂O]=0.033 M, [CO₂]=[H₂]=0.067 M.
questions.solution_summary: Equilibrium calculations using ICE table.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of equilibrium constant and ICE table.

questions.question_text: Explain the rules for calculating oxidation number. OR (i) Write the chemical reactions when borax solution is acidified. (ii) Explain why BF₃ exists whereas BH₃ does not? (iii) SiO₂ is solid but CO₂ is a gas at room temperature.
questions.topic: Physical Chemistry - Redox Reactions / Inorganic Chemistry
questions.sub_topic: Oxidation Number Rules, Borax, BF₃ vs BH₃, SiO₂ vs CO₂
questions.correct_answer: Rules for oxidation number: (1) Free elements have ON 0. (2) Monoatomic ions have ON equal to charge. (3) Oxygen is -2 (except peroxides -1, superoxides -1/2, OF₂ +2). (4) Hydrogen is +1 (except metal hydrides -1). (5) Fluorine is -1. (6) Sum of ON in compound is zero; in ion equals charge. OR (i) Na₂B₄O₇ + 2HCl + 5H₂O → 2NaCl + 4H₃BO₃. (ii) BF₃ has pπ-pπ back bonding from F to B, reducing electron deficiency; BH₃ has no back bonding, so dimerises to B₂H₆. (iii) CO₂ is linear non-polar gas (weak intermolecular forces); SiO₂ has 3D network structure (strong covalent bonds), so solid.
questions.solution_summary: Rules for oxidation numbers; explanations for chemical properties.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multiple concepts: oxidation number rules, back bonding, structure-property relationships.

questions.question_text: (a) In which C-C bond of CH₃CH₂CH₂Br, the inductive effect is expected to be least? (b) Which of the following compound shows geometrical isomerism? (i) Pent-1-ene (ii) Pent-2-ene (iii) 2-Methylbut-2-ene (c) What type of isomerism is present in the following pairs? (i) CH₃CH₂CH₂OH and CH₃CH(OH)CH₃ (ii) CH₃CH₂COCH₂CH₃ and CH₃COCH₂CH₂CH₃ (iii) CH₃CH₂OH and CH₃OCH₃ OR (a) How will you convert ethanoic acid into benzene? (b) "Branched chain hydrocarbons have lower boiling point than straight chain hydrocarbon". Why?
questions.sub_topic: Inductive Effect, Geometrical Isomerism, Isomerism, Conversions
questions.correct_answer: (a) Least inductive effect in C2-C3 bond (farthest from Br). (b) Pent-2-ene shows geometrical isomerism (cis-trans). (c) (i) Position isomerism. (ii) Metamerism. (iii) Functional isomerism. OR (a) CH₃COOH → CH₃COONa → CH₄ (decarboxylation) → CH₃Cl → C₂H₆ → C₂H₅Cl → C₂H₄ → C₂H₄Br₂ → C₂HBr → C₂H₂ → benzene (cyclic polymerisation). (b) Branched chains have smaller surface area, weaker van der Waals forces, lower boiling point.
questions.solution_summary: Inductive effect, geometrical isomerism, isomerism types, conversions.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multiple organic chemistry concepts: inductive effect, isomerism, and conversions.
